name: Even and Odd — The Pairing Game
whatIsIt: An EVEN number can be split into pairs with nobody left out: 8 = four pairs. An ODD number always leaves ONE alone: 7 = three pairs + a lonely one. The fastest check: look at the LAST digit only — 0,2,4,6,8 means even; 1,3,5,7,9 means odd. Even a giant like 3,45,678 confesses through its final digit!
whyNeeded: Pairing shows up everywhere: partners for a dance, sharing between 2 friends, shoes and socks, house numbers on opposite street sides. Even/odd is also your first PATTERN law — numbers alternate even-odd-even-odd forever, and patterns like this power all higher math.
realLifeUses: Class partners: can 27 students pair up evenly? (One left — odd!)
Sharing between 2 siblings: even numbers share perfectly
House numbers: odd on one side of the street, even on the other
Games: 'odd or even?' finger games decide who starts

vocabulary.meaning: Splits into pairs perfectly — ends in 0, 2, 4, 6, or 8.
One is always left unpaired — ends in 1, 3, 5, 7, or 9.
Only the ONES digit decides even/odd — the rest of the number doesn't matter!

story.title: Pairs for the Garba Dance
story.text: The teacher pairs children for the garba dance. With 14 children everyone finds a partner — 7 full pairs — so 14 is EVEN. With 15, poor Ravi is left alone: 15 is ODD! 'For big numbers you need not pair them all,' she says. 'Just look at the LAST digit: 3,45,678 ends in 8, so it is even. The last digit alone decides.'
story.extractedProblem: Are 14 and 15 even or odd? And how can you tell for 3,45,678 without pairing?
story.answerInStory: 14 is even (7 full pairs), 15 is odd (Ravi left alone!). 3,45,678 ends in 8 → even — the last digit alone decides.

visual.caption: Two rows = pairing! 14 fills both rows evenly. 15 leaves one lonely spot — odd.

teachingGallery.title: Even makes pairs; odd has one left over
teachingGallery.note: Line them in twos. No leftover = EVEN. One lonely one = ODD.

standardMethod.summary: Check ONLY the last digit: 0,2,4,6,8 → even; 1,3,5,7,9 → odd.
steps: Look at the ONES digit — ignore everything else!
Why it works: tens/hundreds/thousands are already all pairs — only the ones can break pairing.

alternateMethods.name: Pair-up test (concrete)
alternateMethods.whenToUse: Small numbers, and to SEE what even/odd means.
steps: All matched → even. One alone → odd.

alternateMethods.name: Even-odd arithmetic patterns
steps: even + even = even; odd + odd = EVEN (the two loners pair up!).
Predict, then verify: 23 + 45 → odd+odd → even. Check: 68 ✓
Last-digit spy: look only at the ones digit.
8 is in the even family → 3,45,678 is EVEN. (No pairing of three lakh dancers required!)

workedExamples.problem: Ravi declares 41 even 'because 4 is even'. Which digit should he have watched?
steps: Only the LAST digit decides — the 4 sits in the tens (already all pairs!).
1 is odd → 41 is ODD. Pair-check: 20 pairs + 1 alone ✓
workedExamples.answer: Odd — watch the ones digit, not the loudest one.

trickPractice.intro: Ignore the whole number — the final digit alone tells you even or odd.

hintLadder: Look ONLY at the last digit.

commonMistakes.mistake: Judging by the first digit or any even digit inside (calling 41 even for its 4).
commonMistakes.fix: Only the ONES digit can leave a loner — everything left of it is built of tens, which are pure pairs. Watch the LAST digit only!

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking numbers ending in 0 (like 30, 40) are odd because 'zero is nothing'.
commonMistakes.fix: Ending in 0 means perfectly bundled tens — ALL pairs, nobody alone. 0 belongs to the even family: 10, 20, 30 all split evenly between two friends!

commonMistakes.mistake: Predicting odd + odd = odd ('two odds stay odd').
commonMistakes.fix: The two loners find EACH OTHER! odd + odd = even, always: 3+5 = 8, 7+9 = 16. Only a single unpaired loner makes odd.

easy.q: Can 16 students pair up with nobody left out?

options.label: Even — ending in 0 means all pairs

medium.answer: Even — ending in 0 means all pairs

options.label: Even — the two loners pair up!
Odd — odd plus odd stays odd

medium.answer: Even — the two loners pair up!
hintLadder: Pair with each other → even. Check: 68.

options.label: Even — it ends in 4, sevens don't matter!

challenge.answer: Even — it ends in 4, sevens don't matter!
hintLadder: All those 7s sit in pair-built rooms.
Only the ones digit can leave a loner.

challenge.q: What is the SMALLEST 3-digit even number?

questions.q: Which digit of a number decides even or odd?
hintLadder: Everything else is pairs of tens.

teachBackPrompt: Host a mini dance parade at home: grab 13 small objects, pair them up to show odd, add one more to show even — then teach the last-digit spy trick with a huge number like 5,67,892.

revisionCard.summary: Even = perfect pairs (ends 0,2,4,6,8); odd = one loner (ends 1,3,5,7,9). ONLY the last digit decides — the rest is pre-paired tens! Patterns: odd+odd = even (loners pair up), even+odd = odd. 0-enders are even!

gameMission.title: The Dance Partner Parade
gameMission.brief: Dancers keep arriving in crowds of all sizes! Sort each crowd even/odd with the last-digit spy trick, predict which combined crowds pair perfectly, and find poor Ravi a partner when the count goes odd.

realLifeProject: House-number walk: with a parent, note 6 house numbers on your street. Which side is odd, which even? Then predict even/odd for each family member's age — and verify with the pair-up test using beans!

rememberIt.hook: Even ends in 0,2,4,6,8 (splits in two). Odd ends in 1,3,5,7,9
rememberIt.unpack: Even + even = even, odd + odd = even, even + odd = odd. Only the LAST digit decides.

studentQuestions.q: How do I check a big number like 3458?
studentQuestions.a: Only the last digit matters. 8 is even, so 3458 is even.
Even - it splits into two equal halves and sits between two odd numbers.
